rule.desc: Each class/module should have one reason to change (SRP).
Many small, specific interfaces are better than one large one.
Separate business logic, data access, presentation, and infrastructure.
Pass dependencies as parameters, do not create them internally.
Depend on abstractions (interfaces), not concrete classes.
Open for extension, closed for modification.
Organize modules by feature/domain, not by type.
Keep related functionality together. High cohesion within modules.
Minimize dependencies between modules. Loose coupling preferred.
Prefer flat module structure over deep nesting.
Group many constants in a dedicated module.
Avoid catch-all `utils` modules. Use specific names.
Provide a simple interface to a complex subsystem.
Prefer composition (has-a) over inheritance (is-a).
Convert the interface of one class to another clients expect.
Use object with do-nothing behavior instead of None checks.
Global registry of objects by key.
IoC container managing object creation and dependencies.
Mediates between domain and data mapping layers.
Business rules as composable objects.
Store state changes as sequence of events.
Load and execute code dynamically.
Object wraps database row with data access methods.
All instances share state (alternative to Singleton).
Dynamic dispatch based on two objects' types.
Define family of algorithms, make them interchangeable.
Define subscription mechanism to notify multiple objects of events.
Encapsulate request as object, enabling queuing, logging, undo.
Define algorithm skeleton, let subclasses override steps.
Access elements sequentially without exposing representation.
Change behavior when internal state changes.
Pass request along chain until handled.
Encapsulate how objects interact.
Capture and restore object state.
Separate algorithm from object structure.
Define interface for creating objects, let subclasses decide which class to instantiate.
Create families of related objects without specifying concrete classes.
Construct complex objects step by step.
Ensure class has only one instance. Use sparingly.
Clone objects instead of creating new ones.
Reuse expensive objects from pool.
Defer expensive object creation until needed.
Convert one interface to another clients expect.
Unified interface to set of interfaces in subsystem.
Add behavior to objects dynamically.
Provide placeholder/surrogate for another object.
Compose objects into tree structures.
Share common state to support large numbers of objects.
Decouple abstraction from implementation.
Delete commented-out code. Use version control history.
Use `TODO: description` for temporary markers.
Constants use `UPPER_CASE_WITH_UNDERSCORES`.
Write code that explains itself. Reduce need for comments.
Extract magic numbers to named constants.
Include messages in assertions explaining what failed.
Use clear, descriptive names. Avoid unnecessary abbreviations.
Function names start with verbs.
Class names are nouns.
Boolean variables and functions use `is_`, `has_`, `can_`.
Comments explain *why*, not *what* (code shows what).
Tests should be independent and runnable in any order.
Unit tests should be fast (<1s each).
Extract common test utilities to helper functions or fixtures.
Use doc-tests where supported to validate examples.
Consider property-based testing for complex logic.
Write tests before implementation (Test-Driven Development).
Aim for high coverage of business logic (80%+).
Test names describe what they test and expected behavior.
Tests follow Arrange-Act-Assert structure.
Each test verifies one concept (multiple asserts OK if related).
Use fixtures or shared setup helpers for reusable test data.

Use data-driven tests for multiple inputs and outputs; for first-party Python tests ADR-034 §2/§10 takes precedence (explicit per-scenario methods are the default; parametrize for property/contract suites only).
Mock external dependencies (APIs, databases), not internal logic.
Test full workflows, not just units.
Use realistic test data and include edge cases.

tens.desc: Divergent change, parallel inheritance, temporary fields.
tens.label: Change Smells

rule.desc: Class changes for multiple different reasons.
Subclass doesn't use parent's methods.
Adding subclass in one hierarchy requires adding in another.
Code for future features that aren't needed yet.
Instance variable only used in some methods.

tens.desc: Tight coupling, message chains, data clumps.
tens.label: Coupling Smells

rule.desc: Classes/modules directly depend on concrete implementations.
Module A imports B, B imports A.
One class does everything.
Method uses another class's data more than its own.
Classes know too much about each other's internals.
Long chains like `a.b.c.d.e()`.
Class just delegates to another class.
Same group of parameters passed together everywhere.
Using primitives instead of small objects.
Single change requires modifying many classes.
Implementation details leak through interface.
Mixing high and low-level concepts.
Abstracting before patterns emerge.
Duplicated code that should be abstracted.
Abstraction doesn't cover all cases.
Function with boolean flag changing behavior.
Function with many parameters (>5).
Modifying argument to return value.
Boolean that completely changes function behavior.
Long method chains `a.b().c().d().e()`.
Tests know too much about implementation details.
Tests break on unrelated changes.
Tests depend on each other, must run in order.
Mocking everything including internal logic.
Only happy path tested.
Unit tests take minutes to run.
Tests call private methods directly.
Multiple unrelated asserts, unclear which failed.
Test utilities, fixtures in production code.
Commented-out failing tests.
Unexplained constants in code.
Code nested 5+ levels deep.
Functions > 50 lines.
Similar things named differently.
Old code left commented out.
